value.Title: May 02, 2026: Baker McKenzie Facilitates Virgin Media O2's Renewable Energy Strategy

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-MNG3-RT9K-653P-00000-00May 02, 2026: Baker McKenzie Facilitates Virgin Media O2's Renewable Energy Strategy2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:50:04ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 02, 2026: Baker McKenzie Facilitates Virgin Media O2's Renewable Energy Strategy

Baker McKenzie has successfully advised Virgin Media O2 on its latest long-term Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) with egg Power. This agreement, formalized on the 5th of May 2026, marks a significant step in Virgin Media O2’s commitment to sustainable energy solutions.

Under the 10-year agreement, egg Power will deliver solar energy sourced from its forthcoming solar farm in Suffolk, which is anticipated to commence operations in 2027. The renewable energy supplied through this initiative is projected to account for approximately 5% of Virgin Media O2’s overall energy requirements.

This new PPA is a continuation of Virgin Media O2’s broader clean energy strategy, which includes a previous arrangement for wind energy with The Renewables Infrastructure Group in 2025, also facilitated by Baker McKenzie. Collectively, these two agreements are expected to meet around 20% of the company’s total energy demand, underscoring its commitment to decarbonisation.

The Baker McKenzie team that played a crucial role in this transaction was led by Energy & Infrastructure Partner James Wyatt, alongside Associate Alex Tam, with contributions from Consultant Andrew Hedges and Trainee Kayan Sayeed. Wyatt commented on the deal, stating, 'This agreement represents a further step in Virgin Media O2’s transition towards sustainable energy procurement. We were pleased to support our client on another strategically important PPA, reflecting our continued focus on advising on complex energy and decarbonisation driven projects.'

As companies increasingly prioritize sustainability, this partnership between Virgin Media O2 and egg Power, facilitated by Baker McKenzie, exemplifies the ongoing transition toward renewable energy sources in the corporate sector.

SECTION 1 BAKER MCKENZIE PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

BakerMcKenzie, founded in 1949, is a global law firm headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. With over 6,000 lawyers across 77 offices in 46 countries, it serves clients in energy, technology, and finance, specializing in tax, M&A, and dispute resolution. Generating over $3 billion annually, its a leader in cross-border transactions, advising on global ESG compliance. In 2024, Baker McKenzie enhanced its AI and digital transformation practices, per its website. Its culture emphasizes inclusion, collaboration, and pro bono work, with a top ranking in employee benefits by Legal 500. Competing with DLA Piper, Baker McKenzie stands out for its global reach and local expertise. Recent initiatives include sustainable finance advisory and cloud-based legal tech, aligning with market trends. The Baker & McKenzie Foundation supports community programs, reinforcing its social commitment.

Vision and Mission

Vision

To be acknowledged as the most trusted global law firm, empowering clients to achieve their business goals and spearhead innovation.

Mission

Baker McKenzie aims to help clients traverse the complexities of an evolving world by offering a unique mix of local insights and global context.

Core Values

Bravery -Baker McKenzie embraces courage and innovation, working collaboratively to overcome industry obstacles.

Client Focus -Putting clients first, the firm is dedicated to delivering service excellence.

Inclusion and Diversity -Valuing individuality, Baker McKenzie fosters a culture of empowerment and growth.

Integrity -Conducting business with honesty and transparency, Baker McKenzie builds trust with clients and partners.

Global Perspective -The firm provides a connected approach to business challenges, transcending traditional lines of practice and markets.

urn:contentItem:6JHC-G413-RT9J-20B6-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - THE RECENT SECTOR NEWS INCLUDING PEERS2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:50:04ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - THE RECENT SECTOR NEWS INCLUDING PEERS

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CLAYCO VENTURES INTO RENEWABLE ENERGY WITH NEW SOLAR DIVISION

Chicago-based construction firm Clayco has launched a new business unit focused on large solar energy and storage projects nationwide. This initiative, unveiled on 4th March 2026, is set to establish Clayco Power and Energy, which aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs across the U.S. by 2027, with an anticipated annual revenue of approximately $300 million.Clayco Power and Energy will provide comprehensive services including design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar energy and battery storage systems. Currently, Clayco is developing a solar and battery storage system on a 350-acre site in Manteno, Illinois, approximately an hour south of Chicago, with construction expected to commence this June.The launch aligns with projections of a 15% to 20% increase in U.S. electricity demand by 2030, driven by the rapid growth of data centers, manufacturing facilities, and the electrification of transportation. Notably, Clayco reports that last year, 85% of new electric generating capacity in the U.S. was sourced from solar energy and battery storage, underscoring the urgency of integrating solar solutions into the national energy mix.Clayco CEO Anthony Johnson emphasized the importance of solar energy as part of a comprehensive approach to meet the country’s escalating energy needs, stating, "Solar must be part of the solution. It is the fastest type of power to bring to the market and should work alongside fossil fuels, natural gas, and nuclear energy." This statement reflects the growing consensus on the role of diverse energy sources in ensuring both economic stability and national security.However, Clayco's entry into the solar market arrives amidst challenges posed by recent federal policies that have curtailed incentives for clean energy projects. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act signed by former President Trump has led to significant concerns about job losses in the renewable sector, predicted to reach over 830,000 by 2030. Nevertheless, the passage of Illinois' Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act last year has provided a renewed impetus for solar companies, creating incentives for renewable technologies and energy storage solutions.

+ CLAYCO PARTNERS WITH DEEP ATOMIC FOR PIONEERING NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with the Swiss American energy company Deep Atomic. This partnership involves supporting submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) for the development of the first nuclear-powered AI data center campus in the United States. The proposed facility, which is set to be located at the Idaho National Laboratory, could pave the way for a new era of energy-efficient computing, pending DOE approval.On 12th February 2026, Clayco revealed that it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium's efforts. The firm's extensive experience in delivering large-scale data center projects positions it well to inform the DOE submission process, ensuring that the proposed campus is designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, emphasized the need for confidence in project delivery for successful DOE submissions.In its advisory role, Clayco will also focus on aligning design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads. The firm’s integrated delivery model is aimed at addressing the evolving demands for data centers, which require speed, reliability, and precision. Clayco's participation in this consortium reflects its broader mission to build modern infrastructure capable of supporting America's increasing needs for AI and energy reliability.The consortium brings together various organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's capabilities in practical infrastructure delivery. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. This collaboration aims to establish a scalable and sustainable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure.William G. J. Theron, founder and CEO of Deep Atomic, stated that their mission is to accelerate the deployment of advanced nuclear power solutions to meet the performance and sustainability needs of next-generation computing. With Clayco's involvement, the consortium aims to present a proposal to the DOE that effectively integrates innovative energy solutions with practical execution planning, setting the stage for a transformative project in the U.S. data center landscape.

+ CLAYCO CEO HIGHLIGHTS FOUNTAIN PARK AS A SYMBOL OF RESILIENCE IN NORTH ST. LOUIS

ST. LOUIS, Mo. (First Alert 4) - Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must rally alongside political leaders to create a unified strategy for rebuilding areas that were struggling long before the storm hit. Standing in the Fountain Park neighborhood, Clark acknowledged the slow pace of recovery while expressing optimism about the area’s potential for renewal. “This area particularly, quite devastated, as you know,” Clark said. “I came here right after the tornado and saw the devastation of the trees gone, which is devastating in itself. But what’s even sadder is that many of these homes were being renovated or renovated.”Clark noted that Clayco got to work immediately after the storm hit, coordinating with Mayor Cara Spencer and city services alongside local businesses and community partners. The company’s commitment extends beyond immediate relief to long-term rebuilding efforts, including funding and staffing community programs with partners like Albion and Washington University, all aimed at improving quality of life and keeping the city moving forward. He praised the immediate community response following the tornado, highlighting how residents came together to support each other in the absence of formal plans.Describing Fountain Park as potentially a 'beacon of hope for the rest of the area,' Clark highlighted ongoing developments before the tornado struck. He mentioned city programs like the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority as crucial to promoting new housing and assisting families in finding safe, affordable homes. Clark emphasized the need for comprehensive collaboration, stating, “Everything starts with the civic community.” He believes that a collective will is necessary to navigate the recovery process effectively.Recovery, according to Clark, is a gradual process, drawing parallels to the rebuilding efforts post-Hurricane Katrina. He reiterated the importance of starting somewhere, stating, “Hope springs eternal in the human beast.” Planning for new housing was already underway with the St. Louis Development Corporation before the tornado, and Clark mentioned that efforts to designate blocks for momentum-building projects were in progress. He articulated a vision of incremental progress, reflecting on the phrase, 'one house at a time, one block at a time, one brick at a time.'Despite the challenges, including over 65% of affected property owners not applying for city assistance programs, Clark remains committed to fostering recovery in the region. The city has extended the deadline for its private property assistance program until February 28 to encourage more applications. Clark pointed out the significant improvements visible since the tornado, encouraging a focus on repairing negatives while accentuating positives. He referenced successful transformations such as the Delmar Divine development as

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ RYAN COMPANIES EXPANDS PROPERTY TAX SERVICES WITH MERITAX ADVISORS ACQUISITION

Ryan Companies, a leading global tax services and software provider, has announced the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, a commercial property tax consulting firm based in Texas. This strategic move, made official on the 10th of February 2026, aims to enhance Ryan's property tax capabilities, allowing commercial real estate owners to better navigate the increasingly intricate and technology-driven property tax landscape.Established in 1995, Meritax Advisors has garnered a strong reputation for its expertise in property tax representation across various commercial real estate sectors. The firm manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value for both institutional and private clients, extending its services across Texas and beyond. By integrating Meritax Advisors into its operations, Ryan Companies seeks to bolster its existing property tax services, particularly in valuation strategy, litigation management, and large-portfolio advisory services.Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, emphasized the value of this acquisition, stating, 'With Meritax Advisors joining Ryan, we’re adding a disciplined, client-focused team with deep knowledge of the Texas property tax landscape.' This addition is expected to strengthen Ryan's ability to serve commercial real estate owners not only in Texas but also on a national scale, leveraging the firm's global footprint and proprietary technology.As property tax systems evolve, the demand for advanced analytics and deeper data capabilities becomes critical. Ryan Companies aims to address this need by enhancing Meritax Advisors’ existing advisory model, enabling predictive insights and technology-driven advocacy for clients. Ryan Chismark, a Partner at Meritax Advisors, noted the importance of consistency across jurisdictions and real-time intelligence, highlighting the alignment of values and vision between the two firms.In conjunction with the acquisition, key partners from Meritax Advisors, including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea, and Mark Kline, along with 20 additional team members, will join Ryan. This integration is set to further reinforce Ryan Companies' Property Tax practice, enhancing its offerings in an increasingly competitive market. With over 7,100 professionals serving more than 74,000 clients globally, Ryan continues to solidify its position as the largest firm dedicated exclusively to business taxes.

+ RYAN COMPANIES' ANNA GRIFFIN DRAWS MILITARY INSIGHTS FOR CONSTRUCTION SUCCESS

In a recent episode of the AZ Big Podcast, Anna Griffin, director of construction for Ryan Companies US, shared her unique insights into leadership and resilience shaped by her military background. Recorded 5 days ago, the podcast episode celebrates Veterans Day with Griffin, who has transitioned from serving in the Air Force to managing construction projects across the Southwest.Griffin, who recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan as an aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing, draws parallels between her military service and the construction industry. "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," she noted. "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities."At Ryan Companies, Griffin leads project management teams involved in various sectors, including healthcare, multifamily housing, and education. With nearly four years at the company, her leadership experience is instrumental in navigating the complexities of construction environments. She emphasizes the importance of integrity, accountability, and mission readiness — qualities that were essential during her military service and continue to inform her approach in civilian projects.The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, provides a platform for discussing significant stories in Arizona's business landscape. Sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo, a leading law firm, the podcast aims to highlight how strong leadership and collaboration drive success across various industries. Griffin's episode is particularly timely as Arizona honors its veterans and showcases the valuable skills they bring to the workforce.Listeners are encouraged to tune in to hear Griffin's compelling story and understand how veterans like her are making impactful contributions to industries such as construction. As she continues her work with Ryan Companies, her journey exemplifies the strength and adaptability of veterans transitioning into new roles, reinforcing the idea that the skills honed in military service are invaluable across sectors.

urn:contentItem:6JH4-VGN3-RX8P-623S-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - THE RECENT HEADLINES WITH PEER NEWS2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:50:04ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - THE RECENT HEADLINES WITH PEER NEWS

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CLAYCO VENTURES INTO RENEWABLE ENERGY WITH NEW SOLAR DIVISION

Chicago-based construction firm Clayco has launched a new business unit focused on large solar energy and storage projects nationwide. This initiative, unveiled on 4th March 2026, is set to establish Clayco Power and Energy, which aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs across the U.S. by 2027, with an anticipated annual revenue of approximately $300 million.Clayco Power and Energy will provide comprehensive services including design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar energy and battery storage systems. Currently, Clayco is developing a solar and battery storage system on a 350-acre site in Manteno, Illinois, approximately an hour south of Chicago, with construction expected to commence this June.The launch aligns with projections of a 15% to 20% increase in U.S. electricity demand by 2030, driven by the rapid growth of data centers, manufacturing facilities, and the electrification of transportation. Notably, Clayco reports that last year, 85% of new electric generating capacity in the U.S. was sourced from solar energy and battery storage, underscoring the urgency of integrating solar solutions into the national energy mix.Clayco CEO Anthony Johnson emphasized the importance of solar energy as part of a comprehensive approach to meet the country’s escalating energy needs, stating, "Solar must be part of the solution. It is the fastest type of power to bring to the market and should work alongside fossil fuels, natural gas, and nuclear energy." This statement reflects the growing consensus on the role of diverse energy sources in ensuring both economic stability and national security.However, Clayco's entry into the solar market arrives amidst challenges posed by recent federal policies that have curtailed incentives for clean energy projects. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act signed by former President Trump has led to significant concerns about job losses in the renewable sector, predicted to reach over 830,000 by 2030. Nevertheless, the passage of Illinois' Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act last year has provided a renewed impetus for solar companies, creating incentives for renewable technologies and energy storage solutions.

+ CLAYCO PARTNERS WITH DEEP ATOMIC FOR PIONEERING NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with the Swiss American energy company Deep Atomic. This partnership involves supporting submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) for the development of the first nuclear-powered AI data center campus in the United States. The proposed facility, which is set to be located at the Idaho National Laboratory, could pave the way for a new era of energy-efficient computing, pending DOE approval.On 12th February 2026, Clayco revealed that it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium's efforts. The firm's extensive experience in delivering large-scale data center projects positions it well to inform the DOE submission process, ensuring that the proposed campus is designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, emphasized the need for confidence in project delivery for successful DOE submissions.In its advisory role, Clayco will also focus on aligning design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads. The firm’s integrated delivery model is aimed at addressing the evolving demands for data centers, which require speed, reliability, and precision. Clayco's participation in this consortium reflects its broader mission to build modern infrastructure capable of supporting America's increasing needs for AI and energy reliability.The consortium brings together various organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's capabilities in practical infrastructure delivery. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. This collaboration aims to establish a scalable and sustainable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure.William G. J. Theron, founder and CEO of Deep Atomic, stated that their mission is to accelerate the deployment of advanced nuclear power solutions to meet the performance and sustainability needs of next-generation computing. With Clayco's involvement, the consortium aims to present a proposal to the DOE that effectively integrates innovative energy solutions with practical execution planning, setting the stage for a transformative project in the U.S. data center landscape.

+ CLAYCO CEO HIGHLIGHTS FOUNTAIN PARK AS A SYMBOL OF RESILIENCE IN NORTH ST. LOUIS

ST. LOUIS, Mo. (First Alert 4) - Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must rally alongside political leaders to create a unified strategy for rebuilding areas that were struggling long before the storm hit. Standing in the Fountain Park neighborhood, Clark acknowledged the slow pace of recovery while expressing optimism about the area’s potential for renewal. “This area particularly, quite devastated, as you know,” Clark said. “I came here right after the tornado and saw the devastation of the trees gone, which is devastating in itself. But what’s even sadder is that many of these homes were being renovated or renovated.”Clark noted that Clayco got to work immediately after the storm hit, coordinating with Mayor Cara Spencer and city services alongside local businesses and community partners. The company’s commitment extends beyond immediate relief to long-term rebuilding efforts, including funding and staffing community programs with partners like Albion and Washington University, all aimed at improving quality of life and keeping the city moving forward. He praised the immediate community response following the tornado, highlighting how residents came together to support each other in the absence of formal plans.Describing Fountain Park as potentially a 'beacon of hope for the rest of the area,' Clark highlighted ongoing developments before the tornado struck. He mentioned city programs like the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority as crucial to promoting new housing and assisting families in finding safe, affordable homes. Clark emphasized the need for comprehensive collaboration, stating, “Everything starts with the civic community.” He believes that a collective will is necessary to navigate the recovery process effectively.Recovery, according to Clark, is a gradual process, drawing parallels to the rebuilding efforts post-Hurricane Katrina. He reiterated the importance of starting somewhere, stating, “Hope springs eternal in the human beast.” Planning for new housing was already underway with the St. Louis Development Corporation before the tornado, and Clark mentioned that efforts to designate blocks for momentum-building projects were in progress. He articulated a vision of incremental progress, reflecting on the phrase, 'one house at a time, one block at a time, one brick at a time.'Despite the challenges, including over 65% of affected property owners not applying for city assistance programs, Clark remains committed to fostering recovery in the region. The city has extended the deadline for its private property assistance program until February 28 to encourage more applications. Clark pointed out the significant improvements visible since the tornado, encouraging a focus on repairing negatives while accentuating positives. He referenced successful transformations such as the Delmar Divine development as

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ RYAN COMPANIES EXPANDS PROPERTY TAX SERVICES WITH MERITAX ADVISORS ACQUISITION

Ryan Companies, a leading global tax services and software provider, has announced the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, a commercial property tax consulting firm based in Texas. This strategic move, made official on the 10th of February 2026, aims to enhance Ryan's property tax capabilities, allowing commercial real estate owners to better navigate the increasingly intricate and technology-driven property tax landscape.Established in 1995, Meritax Advisors has garnered a strong reputation for its expertise in property tax representation across various commercial real estate sectors. The firm manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value for both institutional and private clients, extending its services across Texas and beyond. By integrating Meritax Advisors into its operations, Ryan Companies seeks to bolster its existing property tax services, particularly in valuation strategy, litigation management, and large-portfolio advisory services.Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, emphasized the value of this acquisition, stating, 'With Meritax Advisors joining Ryan, we’re adding a disciplined, client-focused team with deep knowledge of the Texas property tax landscape.' This addition is expected to strengthen Ryan's ability to serve commercial real estate owners not only in Texas but also on a national scale, leveraging the firm's global footprint and proprietary technology.As property tax systems evolve, the demand for advanced analytics and deeper data capabilities becomes critical. Ryan Companies aims to address this need by enhancing Meritax Advisors’ existing advisory model, enabling predictive insights and technology-driven advocacy for clients. Ryan Chismark, a Partner at Meritax Advisors, noted the importance of consistency across jurisdictions and real-time intelligence, highlighting the alignment of values and vision between the two firms.In conjunction with the acquisition, key partners from Meritax Advisors, including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea, and Mark Kline, along with 20 additional team members, will join Ryan. This integration is set to further reinforce Ryan Companies' Property Tax practice, enhancing its offerings in an increasingly competitive market. With over 7,100 professionals serving more than 74,000 clients globally, Ryan continues to solidify its position as the largest firm dedicated exclusively to business taxes.

+ RYAN COMPANIES' ANNA GRIFFIN DRAWS MILITARY INSIGHTS FOR CONSTRUCTION SUCCESS

In a recent episode of the AZ Big Podcast, Anna Griffin, director of construction for Ryan Companies US, shared her unique insights into leadership and resilience shaped by her military background. Recorded 5 days ago, the podcast episode celebrates Veterans Day with Griffin, who has transitioned from serving in the Air Force to managing construction projects across the Southwest.Griffin, who recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan as an aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing, draws parallels between her military service and the construction industry. "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," she noted. "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities."At Ryan Companies, Griffin leads project management teams involved in various sectors, including healthcare, multifamily housing, and education. With nearly four years at the company, her leadership experience is instrumental in navigating the complexities of construction environments. She emphasizes the importance of integrity, accountability, and mission readiness — qualities that were essential during her military service and continue to inform her approach in civilian projects.The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, provides a platform for discussing significant stories in Arizona's business landscape. Sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo, a leading law firm, the podcast aims to highlight how strong leadership and collaboration drive success across various industries. Griffin's episode is particularly timely as Arizona honors its veterans and showcases the valuable skills they bring to the workforce.Listeners are encouraged to tune in to hear Griffin's compelling story and understand how veterans like her are making impactful contributions to industries such as construction. As she continues her work with Ryan Companies, her journey exemplifies the strength and adaptability of veterans transitioning into new roles, reinforcing the idea that the skills honed in military service are invaluable across sectors.

urn:contentItem:6JHC-YV13-SHFW-V50N-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Ameren reports 42% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 06 May 2026 09:42 EDT Release Date: May 05, 20262026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:50:04ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Ameren reports 42% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 06 May 2026 09:42 EDT Release Date: May 05, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Ameren (NYSE:AEE), announced net profit of $357m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 42% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 24% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025]. Earnings per share (EPS) were up 38.7% sequentially from 93.0c in Q4/2025 to $1.29 in Q1/2026.

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 23.5% from $289.0m to $357.0m

- Total revenue was up 3.8% from $2.1b to $2.2b

- EPS growth 20.6% from $1.07 to $1.29

- EBITDA to Revenues was up from 33.7% to 37.5%

- EBIT to Revenues was up from 16.2% to 19.2%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was up from 16.2% to 19.2%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was up from 13.8% to 16.4%

Note:

- Depreciation to Revenues was up from 17.5% to 18.3%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Dec 202530 Sep 2025EPS129c93c$2.4Sequential growth in EPS %39-61132Revenue, $ billion2.21.82.7Sequential growth in Revenue %22.1-34.021.5Net Profit, $ million357252641Sequential growth in Net Profit %41.7-61135

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] EPS was up 20.6%, Revenue was up 3.8% and Net Profit was up 23.5%.

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Mar 2025EPS, c129107PCP growth in EPS %219.2Revenue, $ billion2.22.1PCP growth in Revenue %3.815.5Net Profit, $ million357289PCP growth in Net Profit %23.510.7

ACTIVITIES:

Ameren Corporation is a leading utility holding company headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri, serving approximately 2.4 million electric and more than 900,000 natural gas customers across Missouri and Illinois. Its core business activities revolve around the generation, transmission, distribution, and delivery of electricity and natural gas, with operations spanning regulated utilities and infrastructure investments. Ameren focuses on maintaining reliable energy services while emphasizing environmental sustainability and technological advancements. The company's fastest-growing segments include renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar power projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing regulatory support and consumer demand for clean energy. Additionally, investments in electric grid modernization and natural gas infrastructure are driving growth, enhancing efficiency and supporting the integration of emerging technologies like energy storage and smart grids. This strategic direction positions Ameren as a key contributor to the energy transition in the Midwest, balancing economic growth with environmental stewardship.

MARKET POSITION:

Ameren Corporation (NYSE:AEE), the NYSE's 8th largest Utility company by market cap, climbed $14.89 (or 15.4%) in the trailing 12 months to May 05, 2026 to close at $111.64. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 27.6% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -12.3%. Average daily volume in the trailing 12 months equalled average trading of 1.6 million shares in the previous year.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-GWJ3-RVKP-T3VR-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - THE RECENT HEADLINES WITH PEER NEWS2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:50:04ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - THE RECENT HEADLINES WITH PEER NEWS

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CLAYCO VENTURES INTO RENEWABLE ENERGY WITH NEW SOLAR DIVISION

Chicago-based construction firm Clayco has launched a new business unit focused on large solar energy and storage projects nationwide. This initiative, unveiled on 4th March 2026, is set to establish Clayco Power and Energy, which aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs across the U.S. by 2027, with an anticipated annual revenue of approximately $300 million.Clayco Power and Energy will provide comprehensive services including design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar energy and battery storage systems. Currently, Clayco is developing a solar and battery storage system on a 350-acre site in Manteno, Illinois, approximately an hour south of Chicago, with construction expected to commence this June.The launch aligns with projections of a 15% to 20% increase in U.S. electricity demand by 2030, driven by the rapid growth of data centers, manufacturing facilities, and the electrification of transportation. Notably, Clayco reports that last year, 85% of new electric generating capacity in the U.S. was sourced from solar energy and battery storage, underscoring the urgency of integrating solar solutions into the national energy mix.Clayco CEO Anthony Johnson emphasized the importance of solar energy as part of a comprehensive approach to meet the country’s escalating energy needs, stating, "Solar must be part of the solution. It is the fastest type of power to bring to the market and should work alongside fossil fuels, natural gas, and nuclear energy." This statement reflects the growing consensus on the role of diverse energy sources in ensuring both economic stability and national security.However, Clayco's entry into the solar market arrives amidst challenges posed by recent federal policies that have curtailed incentives for clean energy projects. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act signed by former President Trump has led to significant concerns about job losses in the renewable sector, predicted to reach over 830,000 by 2030. Nevertheless, the passage of Illinois' Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act last year has provided a renewed impetus for solar companies, creating incentives for renewable technologies and energy storage solutions.

+ CLAYCO PARTNERS WITH DEEP ATOMIC FOR PIONEERING NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with the Swiss American energy company Deep Atomic. This partnership involves supporting submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) for the development of the first nuclear-powered AI data center campus in the United States. The proposed facility, which is set to be located at the Idaho National Laboratory, could pave the way for a new era of energy-efficient computing, pending DOE approval.On 12th February 2026, Clayco revealed that it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium's efforts. The firm's extensive experience in delivering large-scale data center projects positions it well to inform the DOE submission process, ensuring that the proposed campus is designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, emphasized the need for confidence in project delivery for successful DOE submissions.In its advisory role, Clayco will also focus on aligning design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads. The firm’s integrated delivery model is aimed at addressing the evolving demands for data centers, which require speed, reliability, and precision. Clayco's participation in this consortium reflects its broader mission to build modern infrastructure capable of supporting America's increasing needs for AI and energy reliability.The consortium brings together various organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's capabilities in practical infrastructure delivery. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. This collaboration aims to establish a scalable and sustainable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure.William G. J. Theron, founder and CEO of Deep Atomic, stated that their mission is to accelerate the deployment of advanced nuclear power solutions to meet the performance and sustainability needs of next-generation computing. With Clayco's involvement, the consortium aims to present a proposal to the DOE that effectively integrates innovative energy solutions with practical execution planning, setting the stage for a transformative project in the U.S. data center landscape.

+ CLAYCO CEO HIGHLIGHTS FOUNTAIN PARK AS A SYMBOL OF RESILIENCE IN NORTH ST. LOUIS

ST. LOUIS, Mo. (First Alert 4) - Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must rally alongside political leaders to create a unified strategy for rebuilding areas that were struggling long before the storm hit. Standing in the Fountain Park neighborhood, Clark acknowledged the slow pace of recovery while expressing optimism about the area’s potential for renewal. “This area particularly, quite devastated, as you know,” Clark said. “I came here right after the tornado and saw the devastation of the trees gone, which is devastating in itself. But what’s even sadder is that many of these homes were being renovated or renovated.”Clark noted that Clayco got to work immediately after the storm hit, coordinating with Mayor Cara Spencer and city services alongside local businesses and community partners. The company’s commitment extends beyond immediate relief to long-term rebuilding efforts, including funding and staffing community programs with partners like Albion and Washington University, all aimed at improving quality of life and keeping the city moving forward. He praised the immediate community response following the tornado, highlighting how residents came together to support each other in the absence of formal plans.Describing Fountain Park as potentially a 'beacon of hope for the rest of the area,' Clark highlighted ongoing developments before the tornado struck. He mentioned city programs like the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority as crucial to promoting new housing and assisting families in finding safe, affordable homes. Clark emphasized the need for comprehensive collaboration, stating, “Everything starts with the civic community.” He believes that a collective will is necessary to navigate the recovery process effectively.Recovery, according to Clark, is a gradual process, drawing parallels to the rebuilding efforts post-Hurricane Katrina. He reiterated the importance of starting somewhere, stating, “Hope springs eternal in the human beast.” Planning for new housing was already underway with the St. Louis Development Corporation before the tornado, and Clark mentioned that efforts to designate blocks for momentum-building projects were in progress. He articulated a vision of incremental progress, reflecting on the phrase, 'one house at a time, one block at a time, one brick at a time.'Despite the challenges, including over 65% of affected property owners not applying for city assistance programs, Clark remains committed to fostering recovery in the region. The city has extended the deadline for its private property assistance program until February 28 to encourage more applications. Clark pointed out the significant improvements visible since the tornado, encouraging a focus on repairing negatives while accentuating positives. ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ RYAN COMPANIES EXPANDS PROPERTY TAX SERVICES WITH MERITAX ADVISORS ACQUISITION

Ryan Companies, a leading global tax services and software provider, has announced the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, a commercial property tax consulting firm based in Texas. This strategic move, made official on the 10th of February 2026, aims to enhance Ryan's property tax capabilities, allowing commercial real estate owners to better navigate the increasingly intricate and technology-driven property tax landscape.Established in 1995, Meritax Advisors has garnered a strong reputation for its expertise in property tax representation across various commercial real estate sectors. The firm manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value for both institutional and private clients, extending its services across Texas and beyond. By integrating Meritax Advisors into its operations, Ryan Companies seeks to bolster its existing property tax services, particularly in valuation strategy, litigation management, and large-portfolio advisory services.Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, emphasized the value of this acquisition, stating, 'With Meritax Advisors joining Ryan, we’re adding a disciplined, client-focused team with deep knowledge of the Texas property tax landscape.' This addition is expected to strengthen Ryan's ability to serve commercial real estate owners not only in Texas but also on a national scale, leveraging the firm's global footprint and proprietary technology.As property tax systems evolve, the demand for advanced analytics and deeper data capabilities becomes critical. Ryan Companies aims to address this need by enhancing Meritax Advisors’ existing advisory model, enabling predictive insights and technology-driven advocacy for clients. Ryan Chismark, a Partner at Meritax Advisors, noted the importance of consistency across jurisdictions and real-time intelligence, highlighting the alignment of values and vision between the two firms.In conjunction with the acquisition, key partners from Meritax Advisors, including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea, and Mark Kline, along with 20 additional team members, will join Ryan. This integration is set to further reinforce Ryan Companies' Property Tax practice, enhancing its offerings in an increasingly competitive market. With over 7,100 professionals serving more than 74,000 clients globally, Ryan continues to solidify its position as the largest firm dedicated exclusively to business taxes.

+ RYAN COMPANIES' ANNA GRIFFIN DRAWS MILITARY INSIGHTS FOR CONSTRUCTION SUCCESS

In a recent episode of the AZ Big Podcast, Anna Griffin, director of construction for Ryan Companies US, shared her unique insights into leadership and resilience shaped by her military background. Recorded 5 days ago, the podcast episode celebrates Veterans Day with Griffin, who has transitioned from serving in the Air Force to managing construction projects across the Southwest.Griffin, who recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan as an aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing, draws parallels between her military service and the construction industry. "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," she noted. "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities."At Ryan Companies, Griffin leads project management teams involved in various sectors, including healthcare, multifamily housing, and education. With nearly four years at the company, her leadership experience is instrumental in navigating the complexities of construction environments. She emphasizes the importance of integrity, accountability, and mission readiness — qualities that were essential during her military service and continue to inform her approach in civilian projects.The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, provides a platform for discussing significant stories in Arizona's business landscape. Sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo, a leading law firm, the podcast aims to highlight how strong leadership and collaboration drive success across various industries. Griffin's episode is particularly timely as Arizona honors its veterans and showcases the valuable skills they bring to the workforce.Listeners are encouraged to tune in to hear Griffin's compelling story and understand how veterans like her are making impactful contributions to industries such as construction. As she continues her work with Ryan Companies, her journey exemplifies the strength and adaptability of veterans transitioning into new roles, reinforcing the idea that the skills honed in military service are invaluable across sectors.

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CLAYCO UNVEILS POWER AND ENERGY UNIT AIMING FOR 1,000 JOBS BY 2027

Chicago-based contractor Clayco has formed a new division dedicated to large solar and storage projects across the country. Unveiled on 4th March 2026, the unit, Clayco Power and Energy, targets almost 1,000 U.S. jobs by 2027 and expects roughly $300 million in annual revenue.Clayco Power and Energy will provide design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar and battery storage systems. It is currently developing a 350-acre solar-plus-storage project in Manteno, Illinois, about an hour south of Chicago, with construction due to start this June.The move aligns with a projected rise in electricity demand of 15%–20% by 2030, driven by data centers, manufacturing, and the shift to electrified transportation. Notably, last year saw solar plus storage accounting for the majority of new capacity, underscoring the market tailwinds behind solar deployment.

+ CLAYCO PARTNERS WITH DEEP ATOMIC ON U.S. NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER AT IDAHO NATIONAL LABORATORY

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with Swiss-American energy company Deep Atomic to support submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy for a nuclear-powered AI data center campus planned at Idaho National Laboratory, a project that could usher in a new era of energy-efficient computing.On 12th February 2026, Clayco said it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium. The firm’s track record in delivering large-scale data centers positions it to help ensure the campus can be designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, stressed that confidence in project delivery is essential for a successful DOE submission.Deep Atomic’s approach blends advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to deliver reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. The partnership aims to establish a scalable, sustainable platform that could position the United States as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure; Deep Atomic founder and CEO William G. J. Theron said the mission is to accelerate deployment of advanced nuclear power to meet performance and sustainability needs, with Clayco guiding execution planning for the DOE submission.

+ CLARK URGES UNIFIED ACTION TO REBUILD FOUNTAIN PARK AFTER MAY 16 TORNADO

ST. LOUIS, Mo. (First Alert 4) - Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark told business and civic leaders that rallying with political figures is essential to craft a unified reconstruction strategy for areas already under stress. Standing in Fountain Park, he said the recovery has been slow but the neighborhood shows potential for renewal.Clark said Clayco moved quickly after the storm, coordinating with Mayor Cara Spencer and city services along with local partners. The company’s long-term rebuilding effort includes funding and staffing community programs with Albion and Washington University to improve quality of life and keep the city moving forward. He praised residents for supporting each other in the immediate aftermath in the absence of formal plans.Looking ahead, he described Fountain Park as a beacon of hope and noted developments that were already under way before the tornado, including city programs such as the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority that promote new housing and help families find safe, affordable homes. He stressed that everything starts with the civic community and a shared will to navigate recovery.Clark framed recovery as a gradual process, drawing on parallels to Hurricane Katrina and underscoring the need to start somewhere. He reiterated a vision of incremental progress: one house at a time, one block at a time, one brick at a time.Despite challenges, including more than 65% of affected property owners not applying for city programs, the city extended the deadline for its private property assistance program to February 28 to boost participation. Clark pointed to visible improvements since the tornado and cited the Delmar Divine development as evidence that teamwork can drive meaningful change.

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ RYAN TO BOLSTER PROPERTY TAX CAPABILITIES WITH MERITAX ADVISORS BUY

Ryan Companies has announced the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, a Texas-based commercial property tax consulting firm, with the move made official on the 10th of February 2026. The deal broadens Ryan's property tax capabilities for commercial real estate owners amid a more technology-driven landscape, emphasizing enhancements in valuation strategy, litigation management and large-portfolio advisory services.Meritax Advisors, founded in 1995, has earned a strong reputation for property tax representation across varied commercial real estate sectors and manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value for institutional and private clients in Texas and beyond. The integration is designed to augment Ryan's existing property tax services by bringing deeper analytics, real-time intelligence and predictive insights to support technology-driven advocacy for clients.Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, stated, 'With Meritax Advisors joining Ryan, we’re adding a disciplined, client-focused team with deep knowledge of the Texas property tax landscape.' The firm notes that the acquisition will strengthen Ryan's ability to serve commercial real estate owners not only in Texas but also on a national scale, leveraging Ryan's global footprint and proprietary technology.As part of the integration, key Meritax leaders will join Ryan, including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea and Mark Kline, along with 20 additional team members. The combined organization underscores Ryan’s commitment to expanding its Property Tax practice in a competitive market and aligns with the broader goal of serving more than 74,000 clients worldwide with a 7,100-strong professional team.

+ RYAN COMPANIES' ANNA GRIFFIN APPLIES AIR FORCE EXPERIENCE TO SOUTHWEST CONSTRUCTION PROJECTS

In a new segment of the AZ Big Podcast, Anna Griffin, who oversees construction operations for Ryan Companies US, discussed how leadership and resilience are informed by her military career. The interview was recorded 5 days ago and aligns with Veterans Day by featuring Griffin, who moved from military service to directing construction projects across the Southwest. Griffin had recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan, where she served as aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing. She observed that "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," adding, "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities." At Ryan Companies, she directs project management teams across healthcare, multifamily housing, and education, and, after nearly four years with the firm, her leadership helps maneuver the intricacies of construction sites. She underscores integrity, accountability, and mission readiness as enduring traits from her military days that inform civilian projects. The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, explores notable stories in Arizona's business scene. Sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo, a prominent law firm, the show seeks to illustrate how effective leadership and collaboration propel results across industries. Griffin's appearance arrives at a moment when Arizona honors its veterans and recognizes the marketable skills they bring to the workforce. Listeners are invited to hear Griffin's compelling narrative and learn how veterans like her contribute meaningfully to fields such as construction. As she continues her role with Ryan Companies, her path illustrates the resilience and adaptability of veterans transitioning to new responsibilities, reinforcing that military-honed skills remain valuable across sectors.

urn:contentItem:6JH5-6G93-SC56-B12S-00000-00Cleaner skies ahead with sustainable aviation fuel 2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:41:14ZLexisNexisEDITORIAL OPINIONPg. A,9,999Cleaner skies ahead with sustainable aviation fuel Tax incentives and early investment in SAF could reduce emissions and attract jobs to Massachusetts.By Richard DaveyABSTRACT

Tax incentives and early investment in SAF could reduce emissions and attract jobs to Massachusetts.

BODY

Richard Davey is the chief executive officer of the Massachusetts Port Authority.

When people think about cutting carbon emissions, aviation should top the list.

Flying is one of the hardest sectors to decarbonize. Aircraft cannot simply plug into the grid, and technologies like hydrogen or fully electric planes remain years away from widespread use. The industry needs a solution that works now.

Sustainable aviation fuel is that solution.

Sustainable aviation fuel is an alternative to conventional jet fuel, usable in today's aircraft without changing engines or fueling systems. That means emissions reductions can begin immediately, not decades from now.

SAF has the potential to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 50 percent to more than 80 percent over the life cycle of the fuel. It also cuts particulate pollution, improving air quality in communities near airports. And it can be produced in the United States.

Such fuel can be produced by refining renewable feedstocks — such as used cooking oil, waste fats, and agricultural residues, such as straw or fruit peels, or through a synthetic process by combining carbon with hydrogen.

SAF is less harmful to the environment because of what it's made from. It contains significantly lower amounts of sulfur and particulate matter, and emits less carbon dioxide compared to conventional jet fuel. This reduction results in cleaner engine combustion and improved local air quality around airports.

Despite these advantages, SAF still faces skepticism. Some question whether it can scale to service entire fleets. Others worry about cost, which is more expensive than jet fuel, or unintended consequences, like land-use impacts since SAF can be produced from corn ethanol, which some argue is not a good use of land. These are valid concerns, but they are not reasons to delay action. They are reasons to set strong standards, invest in innovation, and move faster.

The use of SAF could complement ongoing efforts in Massachusetts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions through electrification and the development of more renewable energy sources. At Massport, we are electrifying vehicles, modernizing infrastructure, and designing buildings to use less energy. But the largest share of aviation-related emissions comes from burning jet fuel.

In the current supplemental state budget proposal, Governor Maura Healey and the House of Representatives included a tax incentive for airlines that use SAF in Massachusetts. Initiatives like this could attract more businesses and STEM jobs to the Commonwealth to produce SAF. This is a great first step.

The International Air Transport Association and its members are already moving toward SAF. States and regions that move early are shaping where SAF is produced, blended with conventional jet oil, and distributed, and where the associated jobs and investment ultimately land.

California, Washington, Illinois, and Wisconsin, along with aviation hubs across Europe and Asia, have adopted tax incentives and are building SAF ecosystems that connect production, infrastructure, and workforce development.

Massport helped create a regional working group last year that is partnering with industry and government agencies to help accelerate SAF adoption across New England. Local technology innovators like Sora Fuel and Lydian Labs, based in Cambridge and Charlestown, respectively, are advancing clean fuel technologies that would benefit from policies like a SAF tax credit and could drive future employment and growth associated with the clean energy and technology sectors.

Major US airlines like Delta, American, United, and JetBlue, alongside their international counterparts, are using SAF today and have committed billions to procurement. Those dollars could soon land in Massachusetts.

Smart policies, targeted incentives, clear sustainability standards, and support for regional infrastructure could lower barriers to entry and attract private capital. These actions wouldn't just reduce emissions; they could build durable industries.

SAF is not a silver bullet, and it won't transform aviation overnight. But it offers the most potential for immediate and meaningful emissions reductions, helping both the climate and communities near airports across Massachusetts.

Importantly, SAF won't require travelers to choose between affordability and climate responsibility. It reduces emissions without changing aircraft or safety standards, and without increasing air travel prices.

The SAF industry is still in its early stages, and there is real opportunity for economic growth. If done right, it can be scaled up. The Commonwealth should get in the game.

The path forward is clear: Scale nationwide production, support an SAF hub in the Northeast, and invest with urgency. Cleaner skies are possible using the tools already within our reach.

value.Title: The jet fuel that could (help) save the planet

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-1263-SGB0-S42V-00000-00The jet fuel that could (help) save the planet2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:41:14ZLexisNexisOP-EDSThe jet fuel that could (help) save the planetRichard DaveyABSTRACT

Tax incentives and early investment in SAF could reduce emissions and attract jobs to Massachusetts.

FULL TEXT

Link to Image

Richard Davey is the chief executive officer of the Massachusetts Port Authority.

When people think about cutting carbon emissions, aviation should top the list.

Flying is one of the hardest sectors to decarbonize. Aircraft cannot simply plug into the grid, and technologies like hydrogen or fully electric planes remain years away from widespread use. The industry needs a solution that works now.

Sustainable aviation fuel is that solution.

Sustainable aviation fuel is an alternative to conventional jet fuel, usable in today's aircraft without changing engines or fueling systems. That means emissions reductions can begin immediately, not decades from now.

SAF has the potential to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 50 percent to more than 80 percent over the life cycle of the fuel. It also cuts particulate pollution, improving air quality in communities near airports. And it can be produced in the United States.

Such fuel can be produced by refining renewable feedstocks - such as used cooking oil, waste fats, and agricultural residues, such as straw or fruit peels, or through a synthetic process by combining carbon with hydrogen.

SAF is less harmful to the environment because of what it's made from. It contains significantly lower amounts of sulfur and particulate matter, and emits less carbon dioxide compared to conventional jet fuel. This reduction results in cleaner engine combustion and improved local air quality around airports.

Despite these advantages, SAF still faces skepticism. Some question whether it can scale to service entire fleets. Others worry about cost, which is more expensive than jet fuel, or unintended consequences, like land-use impacts since SAF can be produced from corn ethanol, which some argue is not a good use of land. These are valid concerns, but they are not reasons to delay action. They are reasons to set strong standards, invest in innovation, and move faster.

The use of SAF could complement ongoing efforts in Massachusetts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions through electrification and the development of more renewable energy sources. At Massport, we are electrifying vehicles, modernizing infrastructure, and designing buildings to use less energy. But the largest share of aviation-related emissions comes from burning jet fuel.

In the current supplemental state budget proposal, Governor Maura Healey and the House of Representatives included a tax incentive for airlines that use SAF in Massachusetts. Initiatives like this could attract more businesses and STEM jobs to the Commonwealth to produce SAF. This is a great first step.

The International Air Transport Association and its members are already moving toward SAF. States and regions that move early are shaping where SAF is produced, blended with conventional jet oil, and distributed, and where the associated jobs and investment ultimately land.

California, Washington, Illinois, and Wisconsin, along with aviation hubs across Europe and Asia, have adopted tax incentives and are building SAF ecosystems that connect production, infrastructure, and workforce development.

Massport helped create a regional working group last year that is partnering with industry and government agencies to help accelerate SAF adoption across New England. Local technology innovators like Sora Fuel and Lydian Labs, based in Cambridge and Charlestown, respectively, are advancing clean fuel technologies that would benefit from policies like a SAF tax credit and could drive future employment and growth associated with the clean energy and technology sectors.

Major US airlines like Delta, American, United, and JetBlue, alongside their international counterparts, are using SAF today and have committed billions to procurement. Those dollars could soon land in Massachusetts.

Smart policies, targeted incentives, clear sustainability standards, and support for regional infrastructure could lower barriers to entry and attract private capital. These actions wouldn't just reduce emissions; they could build durable industries.

SAF is not a silver bullet, and it won't transform aviation overnight. But it offers the most potential for immediate and meaningful emissions reductions, helping both the climate and communities near airports across Massachusetts.

Importantly, SAF won't require travelers to choose between affordability and climate responsibility. It reduces emissions without changing aircraft or safety standards, and without increasing air travel prices.

The SAF industry is still in its early stages, and there is real opportunity for economic growth. If done right, it can be scaled up. The Commonwealth should get in the game.

The path forward is clear: Scale nationwide production, support an SAF hub in the Northeast, and invest with urgency. Cleaner skies are possible using the tools already within our reach.

Sustainable aviation fuel is usable in today's aircraft without changing engines or fueling systems.

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CLAYCO UNVEILS POWER AND ENERGY UNIT AIMING FOR 1,000 JOBS BY 2027

Chicago-based contractor Clayco has formed a new division dedicated to large solar and storage projects across the country. Unveiled on 4th March 2026, the unit, Clayco Power and Energy, targets almost 1,000 U.S. jobs by 2027 and expects roughly $300 million in annual revenue.Clayco Power and Energy will provide design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar and battery storage systems. It is currently developing a 350-acre solar-plus-storage project in Manteno, Illinois, about an hour south of Chicago, with construction due to start this June.The move aligns with a projected rise in electricity demand of 15%–20% by 2030, driven by data centers, manufacturing, and the shift to electrified transportation. Notably, last year saw solar plus storage accounting for the majority of new capacity, underscoring the market tailwinds behind solar deployment.

+ CLAYCO PARTNERS WITH DEEP ATOMIC ON U.S. NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER AT IDAHO NATIONAL LABORATORY

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with Swiss-American energy company Deep Atomic to support submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy for a nuclear-powered AI data center campus planned at Idaho National Laboratory, a project that could usher in a new era of energy-efficient computing.On 12th February 2026, Clayco said it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium. The firm’s track record in delivering large-scale data centers positions it to help ensure the campus can be designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, stressed that confidence in project delivery is essential for a successful DOE submission.Deep Atomic’s approach blends advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to deliver reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. The partnership aims to establish a scalable, sustainable platform that could position the United States as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure; Deep Atomic founder and CEO William G. J. Theron said the mission is to accelerate deployment of advanced nuclear power to meet performance and sustainability needs, with Clayco guiding execution planning for the DOE submission.

+ CLARK URGES UNIFIED ACTION TO REBUILD FOUNTAIN PARK AFTER MAY 16 TORNADO

ST. LOUIS, Mo. (First Alert 4) - Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark told business and civic leaders that rallying with political figures is essential to craft a unified reconstruction strategy for areas already under stress. Standing in Fountain Park, he said the recovery has been slow but the neighborhood shows potential for renewal.Clark said Clayco moved quickly after the storm, coordinating with Mayor Cara Spencer and city services along with local partners. The company’s long-term rebuilding effort includes funding and staffing community programs with Albion and Washington University to improve quality of life and keep the city moving forward. He praised residents for supporting each other in the immediate aftermath in the absence of formal plans.Looking ahead, he described Fountain Park as a beacon of hope and noted developments that were already under way before the tornado, including city programs such as the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority that promote new housing and help families find safe, affordable homes. He stressed that everything starts with the civic community and a shared will to navigate recovery.Clark framed recovery as a gradual process, drawing on parallels to Hurricane Katrina and underscoring the need to start somewhere. He reiterated a vision of incremental progress: one house at a time, one block at a time, one brick at a time.Despite challenges, including more than 65% of affected property owners not applying for city programs, the city extended the deadline for its private property assistance program to February 28 to boost participation. Clark pointed to visible improvements since the tornado and cited the Delmar Divine development as evidence that teamwork can drive meaningful change.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ RYAN TO BOLSTER PROPERTY TAX CAPABILITIES WITH MERITAX ADVISORS BUY

Ryan Companies has announced the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, a Texas-based commercial property tax consulting firm, with the move made official on the 10th of February 2026. The deal broadens Ryan's property tax capabilities for commercial real estate owners amid a more technology-driven landscape, emphasizing enhancements in valuation strategy, litigation management and large-portfolio advisory services.Meritax Advisors, founded in 1995, has earned a strong reputation for property tax representation across varied commercial real estate sectors and manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value for institutional and private clients in Texas and beyond. The integration is designed to augment Ryan's existing property tax services by bringing deeper analytics, real-time intelligence and predictive insights to support technology-driven advocacy for clients.Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, stated, 'With Meritax Advisors joining Ryan, we’re adding a disciplined, client-focused team with deep knowledge of the Texas property tax landscape.' The firm notes that the acquisition will strengthen Ryan's ability to serve commercial real estate owners not only in Texas but also on a national scale, leveraging Ryan's global footprint and proprietary technology.As part of the integration, key Meritax leaders will join Ryan, including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea and Mark Kline, along with 20 additional team members. The combined organization underscores Ryan’s commitment to expanding its Property Tax practice in a competitive market and aligns with the broader goal of serving more than 74,000 clients worldwide with a 7,100-strong professional team.

+ RYAN COMPANIES' ANNA GRIFFIN APPLIES AIR FORCE EXPERIENCE TO SOUTHWEST CONSTRUCTION PROJECTS

In a new segment of the AZ Big Podcast, Anna Griffin, who oversees construction operations for Ryan Companies US, discussed how leadership and resilience are informed by her military career. The interview was recorded 5 days ago and aligns with Veterans Day by featuring Griffin, who moved from military service to directing construction projects across the Southwest. Griffin had recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan, where she served as aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing. She observed that "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," adding, "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities." At Ryan Companies, she directs project management teams across healthcare, multifamily housing, and education, and, after nearly four years with the firm, her leadership helps maneuver the intricacies of construction sites. She underscores integrity, accountability, and mission readiness as enduring traits from her military days that inform civilian projects. The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, explores notable stories in Arizona's business scene. Sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo, a prominent law firm, the show seeks to illustrate how effective leadership and collaboration propel results across industries. Griffin's appearance arrives at a moment when Arizona honors its veterans and recognizes the marketable skills they bring to the workforce. Listeners are invited to hear Griffin's compelling narrative and learn how veterans like her contribute meaningfully to fields such as construction. As she continues her role with Ryan Companies, her path illustrates the resilience and adaptability of veterans transitioning to new responsibilities, reinforcing that military-honed skills remain valuable across sectors.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
